name: Headless commerce: Vendure + Next.js
description: Headless e-commerce template with Vendure (GraphQL, Stripe, PostgreSQL, Redis) and a Next.js storefront—structured for typed API contracts, secret hygiene, and reproducible container deploys with TLS at the edge.
longDescription: A commerce reference implementation pairing Vendure on Node.js/TypeScript with a Next.js storefront. The commerce layer exposes GraphQL Shop and Admin APIs, supports Stripe (cards, MB WAY, Multibanco and related local payment methods where configured), EU VAT/B2B-oriented custom fields, and S3/MinIO-backed assets. Separation between the admin API, shop API, and storefront keeps permissions and caching strategies explicit. The storefront uses SSR, Tailwind, shadcn/ui, TanStack Query, and Zustand with a clear client–server boundary for data fetching. Deployment is container-first with compose files, health-checked services, Caddy as reverse proxy in production for automatic HTTPS, and a Windows manage.ps1 console for repeatable build and lifecycle tasks—reducing drift between environments.
features: Multi-channel commerce core with orders, products, and admin API
Next.js storefront with theme contract and Vendure GraphQL client
Stripe payment flows and asset storage integration
GraphQL schema as the integration contract between storefront and commerce core
Development and production compose stacks with documented environment separation
Caddy configuration for automatic HTTPS where a domain is available
manage.ps1-driven workflows for build, deploy, and packaging
highlights: End-to-end headless pattern: typed GraphQL boundary from catalog through checkout
Defense in depth: TLS at the proxy, secrets only via env, webhook flows treated as privileged
Operational scripts and compose stacks that mirror how Windows-based teams run containers in practice
challenges: Headless commerce requires tight alignment between storefront UX, payment webhooks, and long-lived database migrations across Vendure upgrades.
solutions: Keep vendure and storefront in one workspace with shared tooling, health-checked compose services, documented environment separation for dev vs prod, and explicit notes on webhook verification and database migration discipline across upgrades.
security: Secrets and keys only via environment configuration—never committed defaults
Reverse proxy and TLS termination documented for production shapes; minimise exposed admin surfaces
Stripe and webhook configuration treated as sensitive operations with rotation guidance
PostgreSQL and Redis network exposure limited to the compose network by default
Asset buckets (S3/MinIO) configured with least-privilege credentials where documented
